name: Middle Winterslow
overview: Middle Winterslow is one of the linked Winterslow villages about 6 miles north-east of Salisbury, lying just south of the A30 London Road on the old Roman road between Old Sarum and Winchester. It is the service centre of the roughly 2,000-strong Winterslow parish, holding the primary school, the village shop and Post Office, the main pub, the doctors' surgery, a church and the modern village hall. The Clarendon Way long-distance footpath runs through the village.
character: A settled, community-minded chalk-downland village strung along quiet lanes, mixing older flint-and-brick cottages with post-1960s housing amid surrounding farmland and woodland.
amenities: One Stop village shop with full Post Office (Middleton Road)
The Lord Nelson Arms pub and brasserie (Middleton)
Winterslow Village Hall with tennis court and recreation grounds
Cricket club and a range of village clubs/societies
Parish church plus Methodist and Baptist chapels

schools.name: Winterslow Church of England Primary School

commutes.notes: Roughly 6-9 miles into the city via the A30/London Road.
From Salisbury (about 9 miles) on SWR; Grateley station (about 8 miles) reaches Waterloo in around 85 min. Dean is the nearest station (request stop, about 5 miles) but has a sparse service.
Via the A30 for A303 connections east/west.

prices.summary: Sold prices across Winterslow averaged roughly £520,000 in the year to late 2025 - well above the Salisbury town average of about £338,000 - reflecting a market led by detached houses and period cottages. Smaller terraces and ex-local-authority homes (e.g. around Saxon Leas) start in the high £200,000s, while larger houses on lanes such as Middleton can exceed £700,000.

thingsToDo: Walk or cycle the Clarendon Way long-distance path through the village
Visit the New Art Centre sculpture park and gallery at Roche Court, East Winterslow
Explore Bentley Wood on the parish edge, known for butterflies and woodland birds
Sunday lunch at a traditional village pub
placesToEat: The Lord Nelson Arms (pub and brasserie, Middleton)
The Lion's Head at The Common
pros: A genuine working village with a primary school, shop/Post Office, pub, surgery and active community life
Quick access to the A30 and A303 and to Salisbury's mainline station for London commuting
Surrounded by chalk downland, the Clarendon Way and Bentley Wood for walking and wildlife
cons: A strung-out layout means some homes are a walk from the shop and school, and retail is limited to a convenience store
Car-dependent: the nearest mainline station and full supermarkets are several miles away in Salisbury or at Grateley
House prices sit well above the Salisbury average
whoItSuits: Families and downsizers who want a real village community with a school and pub within easy reach of Salisbury and good A-road links, and who don't mind relying on a car.
